summary:
Rome's conquest of Greece (214–146 BC) was a gradual process culminating in the decisive Battle of Pydna (168 BC), which ended the Macedonian kingdom, and the sack of Corinth (146 BC), which effectively completed Roman control of the Greek peninsula. Rome's initial interventions were framed as protecting Greek liberty from Macedonian hegemony — the 'Freedom of the Greeks' declared at the Isthmian Games in 196 BC was genuinely celebrated by many Greeks — but over the following decades it became clear that Roman 'protection' meant Roman dominance.

The Macedonian Wars proceeded in stages: the First and Second Macedonian Wars (214–205 BC and 200–196 BC) culminated in the defeat of Philip V; the Third Macedonian War (171–168 BC) ended with the victory at Pydna and the humiliation of King Perseus. The [[event:1203|Achaean League]] revolted in 146 BC and was crushed; Corinth, one of the wealthiest Greek cities, was razed and its population enslaved as an exemplary punishment. The cultural consequence was paradoxical: Rome absorbed Greek art, literature, philosophy, and religion on a vast scale. As Horace later wrote, 'Conquered Greece took captive her savage conqueror' — the Greek cultural tradition became the foundation of Roman elite education and self-identity.
